1: “Wine (yayin) is a mocker (lēṣ) and strong drink (šēkār) a brawler (hōmeh); whoever is led astray (šōgeh) by it is not wise.” The personification of wine as a lēṣ (scoffer/mocker — the most dangerous human type in Proverbs) gives the substance the characteristics of the most destructive human character. Hāmāh (to roar, to be boisterous — used of the crowds, the sea, the city; cf. 1:21; 9:13; Ps 46:3) — strong drink makes men riotous. Šāgāh (to stagger, go astray — as in 19:27) — the drunkard wanders from the path of wisdom.
2: “The terror (ʾêmat) of a king is like the roaring (kᵉnaham) of a lion (kᵉpîr); whoever provokes him to anger (mitʿabbᵉrô) puts his own life (napšô) at risk (ḥôṭēʾ).” Nearly identical to 19:12, but here the focus falls on the danger of provoking royal wrath — ʿābar in the hithpael (to make oneself pass over, to become enraged) describes the king going beyond normal limits in anger. Ḥāṭāʾ (to sin, to miss — used in 19:2 for missing the path) — provoking the king is to sin against one’s own life.
3: “It is an honor (kābôd) for a man to stay out of conflict (šebet mêrîb), but every fool (ʾewîl) will be drawn into quarreling (yitgallāʿ).” Šebet (sitting, staying — the quiet of non-engagement) in contrast to rîb (strife, lawsuit, quarrel — the legal word for dispute). Gālaʿ (to quarrel, to break out — hithpael: to involve oneself in a brawl) — the fool cannot resist the pull of a fight. Honor (kābôd) in Proverbs is consistently given to restraint and wisdom, not to aggressive self-assertion.
4: “The sluggard does not plow (lōʾ-yaḥᵃrōš) in the cold season (ḥōrep); at harvest (qāṣîr) he will look for food and find nothing.” Ḥōrep (autumn, the cold-wet season — the plowing season in Palestine) — the window for sowing is also the uncomfortable season. The sluggard’s avoidance of seasonal labor forfeits the harvest. The agricultural calendar is moralized: refusal to work in the hard season produces hunger in the good season. The verse is one of the most concrete images of the consequences of sloth.
5: “The counsel (ʿēṣāh) in a man’s heart (bᵉleb-ʾîš) is like deep water (mayim ʿᵃmûqqîm), but a man of understanding (ʾîš tᵉbûnāh) draws it out (yidlennāh).” Deep water requires a drawing-vessel to access; wisdom in the heart requires another wise person to draw it out. Dālāh (to draw water — the word used by the daughters of Reuel in Exod 2:16) applied to drawing out a person’s inner wisdom. The image commends the kind of deep listening that extracts wisdom others cannot reach themselves.
6: “Many people proclaim (yiqrāʾ) their own loyalty (ḥasdô), but a truly faithful person — who can find (mî yimṣāʾ)?” The rhetorical question exposes the gap between claimed and actual ḥesed. Ḥesed (steadfast love, faithful loyalty) is the highest covenant virtue — many claim it, few embody it. The question “who can find?” echoes the search for wisdom herself (8:17) and the capable wife (31:10), placing faithful love in the category of rare and precious discoveries.
7: “A righteous man (ṣaddîq) who walks in his integrity (bᵉtummô) — blessed are his children (ʾašrê bānāyw) after him.” The ʾašrê (beatitude) formula applies not to the righteous man himself but to his children — they inherit the blessedness of their father’s integrity. Tōm (integrity — cf. 19:1) is the quality of wholeness that generates generational blessing (cf. Ps 112:1–2). The father’s righteousness radiates outward into his posterity.
8: “A king who sits (yōšēb) on the throne of judgment (kissēʾ-dîn) scatters (mᵉzāreh) all evil with his eyes (bᵉʿênāyw).” Zārāh (to scatter, winnow — the act of throwing grain into the air to separate wheat from chaff; cf. Jer 15:7; 31:10) applied to the king’s judicial discernment. The eyes that “scatter evil” are the penetrating gaze of the just judge who exposes and disperses wickedness by the mere act of righteous seeing. The king as winnower is a significant image for divine judgment (Matt 3:12).
9: “Who can say, ‘I have made my heart pure (zikkîtî libbî); I am clean (ṭāhartî) from my sin’?” The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Zākāh (to be pure, clean — the root of zāk, pure; cf. Job 15:14–16; 25:4) and ṭāhēr (to be ritually/morally clean) — both purity terms. The verse is the closest thing in Proverbs to a doctrine of universal sinfulness (cf. Ps 51:5; Eccl 7:20). No one can truthfully claim self-achieved moral purity; the honest person knows this about themselves.
10: “Unequal weights (ʾeben wāʾāben) and unequal measures (ʾêpāh wᵉʾêpāh) — both are detestable (tôʿᵃbat) to the LORD.” Two stones (weights) and two ephahs (dry measures) — the rigged scale. Tôʿēbāh (abomination — the strongest negative assessment term; cf. 3:32; 11:1; 12:22) places commercial fraud in the category of the most offensive sins against God. The Mosaic law is explicit (Lev 19:35–36; Deut 25:13–16; Amos 8:5); Proverbs repeats the condemnation in 11:1; 20:23.
11: “Even a child (naʿar) is known (yitnakkēr) by his actions (bᵉmaʿᵃlālāyw), whether his conduct is pure (zāk) and right (yāšār).” Nākar (hithpael: to make oneself known, to be recognized — cf. 26:24; Gen 42:7) — even at the child level, behavior reveals character. Maʿᵃlāl (deed, action — usually of significant or evil acts; cf. Isa 1:16; Jer 4:4; Ps 77:11) — a person’s deeds are their introduction. The verse commends early character formation: the child already has a moral track record.
12: “The hearing ear (ʾōzen šōmaʿat) and the seeing eye (ʿayin rōʾāh) — the LORD made them both.” A creation-theology observation: the faculties by which wisdom enters the person — hearing and sight — are divine gifts. They carry an implicit obligation: organs made by God for wisdom-reception should be used for that purpose (cf. 17:24; Ps 94:9; Isa 6:9–10). The verse also grounds epistemology in creation: our capacity to perceive truly is a gift to be honored.
13: “Do not love sleep (ʾal-tʾehab šēnāh), or you will come to poverty (tiwwārēš); keep your eyes open (pᵉqaḥ ʿênekā) and you will have plenty of food (tiśbaʿ-lāḥem).” The sleep-love of the sluggard (cf. 6:9–11; 24:33–34; 19:15) produces poverty. Pāqaḥ (to open the eyes — the same word as when Hagar’s eyes were opened in Gen 21:19; when blind eyes are opened in Isa 35:5) — alertness is the opposite of sloth, and it produces abundance (śābēaʿ, fullness).
14: “Bad, bad (raʿ raʿ), says the buyer, but when he goes away, he boasts (yithallel) of his deal.” A scene from marketplace psychology — the buyer deprecates the merchandise to drive down the price, then brags about the bargain when safely away. No moral condemnation is explicit; the verse is an observation about commercial human nature and the strategic use of speech. Hāllal (hithpael: to boast — cf. 27:1–2; Ps 34:2) — self-congratulation in private after deceptive speech in public.
15: “There is gold (zāhāb) and plenty of rubies (pᵉnînîm), but the lips that speak knowledge (śiptê-daʿat) are the truly precious thing (kᵉlî yāqār).” The three-level comparison: gold, rubies (already at the top of material value), and speech-that-gives-knowledge. Kᵉlî yāqār (a precious vessel, a rare implement) — knowledge-bearing speech is rarer and more valuable than the rarest physical commodity. The image carries the same rhetorical weight as 3:14–15 and 8:10–11.
16: “Take the garment (beged) of one who puts up security (ʿārab) for a stranger; hold it in pledge (baḥᵃbōlēhû) for those who are surety for foreigners.” The practical wisdom of collateral: surety for strangers is high-risk lending (cf. 6:1–5; 11:15; 17:18). ʿĀrab (to mix oneself in, to become surety — cf. Gen 43:9) — pledging your own property for another’s debt. The advice is essentially: insist on security from reckless pledgers. The verse is repeated nearly verbatim in 27:13.
17: “Bread (leḥem) gained by deceit (šeqer) is sweet (yiʿᵃrab) to a man, but afterward (ʾaḥᵃrîtô) his mouth will be full of gravel (ḥāṣāṣ).” The temporal structure — sweet now, grit later — is the standard Proverbs pattern for folly’s attractive lies (cf. 5:3–4 for the adulteress). ʿĀreb (pleasant to the taste — cf. 3:24; 13:19) versus ḥāṣāṣ (gravel, small stones — what results when food turns to grit). Deceit masquerades as sweetness; its exposure fills the mouth with the inedible.
18: “Plans (maḥᵃšābôt) are established by seeking advice (bᵉʿēṣāh), and with wise guidance (taḥbulôt) you can wage war (taʿᵃśeh milḥāmāh).” Maḥᵃšābāh (plan — cf. 19:21) requires communal wisdom to be sound. Taḥbulôt (guidance, skillful steering — the nautical term from 1:5) now appears in a military context: strategic planning requires the wisdom of many counselors (cf. 11:14; 15:22; 24:6). The wisdom community, not the lone genius, is the source of reliable strategy.
19: “The gossip (hôlēk rākîl) reveals secrets (gôleh-sôd) wherever he goes; avoid anyone who talks too freely (ʿim-pōteh śᵉpātāyw lōʾ titʿārab).” Rākîl (slander, gossip — from rākal, to go about trading secrets; cf. Lev 19:16; Jer 6:28) — the gossip trades in other people’s private information. Pātāh śᵉpātāyw (one who opens his lips — talks freely, without restraint) — the advice is relational: avoid entering into confidences with those who cannot keep them (cf. 11:13; 18:8).
20: “Whoever curses (mᵉqallel) his father or his mother — his lamp (nērô) will be snuffed out (yidʿak) in deepest darkness (bᵉʾîšôn ḥōšek).” Qālal (piel: to curse, to treat as trivial and contemptible — the opposite of kābēd, to honor; Exod 21:17) — the fifth commandment violation inverted. Nēr (lamp — the life-symbol; cf. 13:9; Job 18:6; 21:17) extinguished “in the deepest darkness (ʾîšôn = the pupil of the eye, the darkest center)” — a particularly evocative phrase for total obliteration.
21: “An inheritance (naḥᵃlāh) quickly gained (mᵉbōhelet) at first will not be blessed (lōʾ tᵉbōrak) in the end.” Bāhal (pual: to be hastened, seized prematurely) — the inheritance obtained ahead of its proper time (by manipulation, impatience, or the death-wish toward parents) is cursed rather than blessed. The principle mirrors 13:11 (“wealth gotten hastily will dwindle”). Patient reception of what comes in due course is contrasted with greedy seizure of what is not yet yours.
22: “Do not say, ‘I will repay (ʾᵃšallem-rāʿāh) evil’; wait on (qawwēh) the LORD and he will deliver you (wᵉyōšaʿ-lāk).” Qāwāh (to wait, to hope — the same root as tiqwāh, hope, in 10:28; 11:7; 13:12) — the posture of patient trust rather than active vengeance. “He will deliver you (yōšaʿ)” — the salvation-verb, from the root of Yeshua/Joshua. The proverb anticipates the NT teaching on non-retaliation (Rom 12:17–19, which quotes Deut 32:35; cf. Matt 5:39).
23: “Unequal weights (ʾeben wāʾāben) are detestable (tôʿᵃbat) to the LORD, and dishonest scales (mōʾzᵉnê mirmāh) are no good (lōʾ-ṭôb).” A near-repetition of v10, using slightly different vocabulary. Mirmāh (deceit, fraud — cf. Ps 5:6; Zeph 1:9) combined with mōʾzᵉnayim (scales, balance) — the dishonest scale embodies deceptive commercial practice. The repetition reinforces the absolute nature of the condemnation: commercial fraud is not a minor infraction but a divine abomination.
24: “A man’s steps (miṣʿᵃdê-gāber) are directed by the LORD (mēYHWH); how then can anyone understand (yābîn) his own path (darkô)?” Gāber (a strong man, a warrior — cf. Job 3:3; 38:3) — even the strong man’s steps are divinely directed. The rhetorical question acknowledges the limits of human self-understanding: if God directs your steps, you cannot from your vantage point trace the full pattern of your own path. This does not produce fatalism but humility (cf. 16:9; 19:21).
25: “It is a snare (mōqēš) for a man to dedicate something rashly (yālaʿ qōdeš) and only afterward to reconsider (ʾaḥar nᵉdārîm lᵉbaqqēr).” yālaʿ (to swallow up, here in the sense of rash vowing — cf. Eccl 5:2–6; Num 30) — a hasty dedication to God becomes a trap when one later has second thoughts. Neder (vow — solemn promise to God; Num 30:2; Deut 23:21–23) cannot be undone casually. The wisdom is: think before you vow.
26: “A wise king (melek ḥākām) scatters (mᵉzāreh) the wicked and drives (wayyāšeb) the threshing wheel (ʾôpān) over them.” The winnowing image of v8 reappears: the just king’s justice is agricultural separation — wicked chaff from righteous grain. ʾôpān (wheel, threshing wheel) — the heavy wheel rolled over grain to separate it. The violence of the image (the wicked crushed by the threshing wheel) underscores that wise kingship cannot be purely passive benevolence; it must actively deal with wickedness.
27: “The human spirit (nišmat ʾādām) is the LORD’s lamp (nēr-YHWH), searching (ḥōpēś) all the innermost parts (ḥadrê-bāṭen) of a person.” Nišmāh (breath, spirit — cf. Gen 2:7 where God breathed nišmat ḥayyîm into Adam) — the divine life-breath in the human person becomes God’s lamp for illuminating the inner self. Ḥōpēś (searching, probing — cf. Jer 17:10; Ps 139:1) — the divine-origin spirit searches the deepest chambers of the person’s interior. Conscience is here understood as the divine light within the human frame.
28: “Steadfast love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾĕmet) preserve (yiṣṣᵉrû) the king, and his throne is upheld (sāmûk) by faithful love (ḥesed).” The paired covenant-character terms of Exod 34:6–7 and Prov 3:3 now applied to royal governance: the king who embodies ḥesed-wĕʾĕmet secures his throne. ḥesed (steadfast love) appears twice — framing the verse — emphasizing that covenant loyalty is the foundation, not military power or political cunning (cf. 29:14; Ps 89:14).
29: “The glory (tipʾeret) of young men (bᵉḥûrîm) is their strength (kōḥām), but the honor (hadar) of old men (zᵉqēnîm) is their gray hair (śêbāh).” A complementarity observation: each stage of life has its appropriate glory. Tipʾeret (splendor, beauty, glory — cf. 4:9; Isa 28:1; Ezek 16:12) for the vigor of youth; hādār (dignity, majesty — cf. Ps 96:6; 111:3) for the experienced elder. Śêbāh (gray hair — cf. 16:31; Lev 19:32) is the badge of the zāqēn (elder/elders), who earned both the title and the dignity through years of lived wisdom.
30: “Painful blows (ḥabburvôt-pāreʿ) cleanse away (yitmᵉraq) evil (bᵉrāʿ); wounds (makkôt) reach the innermost parts (ḥadrê-bāṭen) of a person.” Ḥabbûrāh (welt, bruise — cf. Isa 1:6; 53:5) and makkāh (blow, wound) — physical correction has a purifying effect (yitmᵉraq = to be scoured clean, polished). “Reaches the innermost parts” (ḥadrê-bāṭen — the same phrase as v27) — the correction that scours the surface also reaches the deepest interior. Discipline penetrates where words cannot.
